metadata.title: How to Actually Find Companies That Sponsor International Students

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Most companies genuinely don't understand sponsorship, so the pool of true sponsors is smaller than it looks. The 80 Days to Stay framing captures how little time graduates have to act. Filings increasing year-over-year earn a company plus ten points as a sponsorship signal. An HR person who previously handled visas is a clue that a company can navigate the process.

artifactLines: Most companies genuinely don't understand sponsorship, so the pool of true sponsors is smaller than it looks.
The 80 Days to Stay framing captures how little time graduates have to act.
Filings increasing year-over-year earn a company plus ten points as a sponsorship signal.
An HR person who previously handled visas is a clue that a company can navigate the process.

beats.narration_text: The scoring idea, where filings increasing year-over-year adds ten points, plus the '80 Days to Stay' urgency, showed me sponsorship-hunting is a data problem not a hope problem. I want to build my own version. Help me: (1) pull a list of employers with recent visa filings and score them by trend; (2) find one signal, like a returning HR person who once handled visas, that raises a company's odds; (3) plan a realistic pace to apply meaningfully within that eighty-day window.
